title: Spotting the Fakes
subtitle: The many faces of fraud

hook.metaphor: Not every fake at the door looks the same. Some have a forged ID (a stolen identity). Some borrowed a regular's wristband (a hijacked account). Some are perfectly real people who'll cause trouble inside on purpose (first-party fraud). And some smooth-talk the bouncer into just letting them in (a scam). Spotting fraud means knowing the whole cast.
hook.scenario: A customer calls, distressed: she got a call from 'her bank's fraud team', who convinced her to move her savings to a 'safe account' to protect them. She authorised the transfer herself. Was she defrauded — even though she pressed the buttons?

learn: **Identity fraud** is using someone else's identity (real or invented) to open accounts or borrow. **Synthetic identity** — stitching real and fake details into a brand-new 'person' — is a fast-growing, hard-to-catch variant.

**Account takeover (ATO)** is hijacking a *genuine* customer's existing account — via phishing, stolen passwords, or SIM-swapping. It's dangerous precisely because the account is real and trusted; the criminal wears the customer's face.

**First-party fraud** flips the script: the fraudster is the *real* account holder, lying for gain — inflating income on an application, or 'first-party' chargeback abuse ('I never got it' when they did). No stolen identity, just dishonesty by a genuine customer.

And the fastest-growing category: **scams**, especially **Authorised Push Payment (APP) fraud** — exactly our scenario. The victim is manipulated into *authorising the payment themselves*, so old defences ('was this the real customer?') don't trigger — it *was* her. APP scams (impersonation, romance, investment, invoice redirection) have exploded, and regulators in the UK, Australia and beyond are pushing firms toward shared responsibility and even reimbursement. The hard truth: yes, she was defrauded — consent obtained by deception is still fraud.
bridge: So at work, you can stop picturing fraud as just 'a stolen card.' Ask: *Is the identity fake (identity/synthetic fraud)? Is a real account hijacked (ATO)? Is the genuine customer lying (first-party)? Or has the customer been manipulated into doing it themselves (a scam/APP)?* Each needs a different defence — and the scam category is the one quietly winning right now.

interactions.title: Did she 'authorise' it?
interactions.prompt: The customer moved her own savings after a convincing impersonation call. How should the firm see it?

options.label: Her fault — she authorised it, so it's not fraud.
options.feedback: This is the outdated view. Consent obtained by deception is still fraud (APP scam). Blaming victims both misreads the crime and increasingly breaches regulators' reimbursement expectations.

options.label: An APP scam — she was deceived into authorising it; treat it as fraud.
options.feedback: Correct. Authorised Push Payment fraud is the fastest-growing category precisely because the victim presses the buttons. The deception is the crime.

options.label: Money laundering, not fraud.
options.feedback: No money was being *disguised* here — she was *deceived* into paying. That's fraud. (The criminal will likely launder the proceeds next — a separate step.)

interactions.title: Know the cast
interactions.prompt: Match each fraud type to its description.

pairs.right: Using someone else's (or a fake) identity to transact
A fabricated 'person' built from real + fake details
Hijacking a genuine customer's existing account
The real account holder lying for gain
Manipulating a victim into authorising the payment themselves

interactions.title: Whose identity is in play?
interactions.prompt: Sort each case by whether a real, fake, or hijacked identity is involved.

items.label: Real account holder inflates income on a loan form
Criminal logs into a victim's real bank account
A 'person' built from mixed real and fake details
Victim tricked into transferring her own money
Loan opened using a stolen driver's licence

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “Fraud isn't one thing — it's fake identities, hijacked accounts, dishonest real customers, and (fastest of all) scams where the victim is tricked into paying themselves.”

checkpoint.q: In Authorised Push Payment (APP) fraud, the victim…
options: Is deceived into authorising the payment themselves
checkpoint.explain: The victim presses the buttons under manipulation — which is why old 'is this the real customer?' checks don't catch it.

checkpoint.q: Account takeover (ATO) is dangerous mainly because…
options: It uses an obviously fake account
The account is genuine and trusted, so the criminal wears the customer's face
checkpoint.explain: Hijacking a real, trusted account lets the criminal bypass defences aimed at fake identities.

checkpoint.q: A genuine customer inflating their income on an application is…
checkpoint.explain: First-party fraud = the real account holder being dishonest for gain, no stolen identity involved.
